summary: DHH celebrates what he sees as the collapse of progressive social accusations (racist, misogynist, fascist) as effective political tools, using the 2024 US presidential election as evidence. He argues these labels were overused to the point of meaninglessness, comparing them to rubber checks that eventually bounce. He frames the decline of DEI and 'woke orthodoxy' as the end of a preference falsification where people went along with prevailing norms out of fear. He concludes this is a universal win regardless of one's opinion on Trump.
key_points: Accusations of racism, misogyny, and fascism have lost their power through overuse and lack of substantiation
The DEI movement and critical-theory thinking were amplified by preference falsification — people conforming publicly while disagreeing privately
The 2024 presidential election result proves these social pressure tactics have lost their political effectiveness
What appeared to be widespread consensus was actually distorted perception — 'shadows on the wall' made larger by the angle of light
money_quotes: They just don't work any more, those baseless accusations that anyone we disagree with is a racist, misogynist, fascist.
The problem with accusations like that is that they eventually have to be backed by proof or they'll bounce like a rubber check.
When even the most mundane political or moral positions were able to earn one of those *ist-y labels, the entire enterprise of throwing them around was bound to go bankrupt.

Being professionally offended by every little thing, seeing micro-aggressions around every corner, and rendering the entire world through a narrow oppressed/oppressor lens is back to the niche disposition once more.
This particular preference falsification, where people just go along with what's in vogue to keep their head down, has left the zeitgeist.
That was shadows on the wall, distorted to thrice their size from the angle of the light.
That big scary mob that once was has been reduced to near impotence in most arenas, including the biggest of all, the US presidential election.
key_insight: The overuse of social stigma labels without substantiation has bankrupted their credibility, and the 2024 election proved the cultural moment that sustained them has passed.
